replies.title: Re: Gregorian Chant Handbooks
replies.content:
As someone who is musically inclined, I wonder if there's a way to experiment with this directly. Gregorian Chants are based on Medieval modes (scales), so there could be a lot of unique tonal patterns.

The thought of singing these, even alone, makes me very anxious. Does anyone have any thoughts on whether something like this could work on a keyboard?

replies.title: Re: Gregorian Chant Handbooks
replies.content: You can certainly play Gregorian tunes on an instrument. The mountain dulcimer is a modal instrument, and I've played various modal pieces on mind. I know of no reason you couldn't do the same thing, and get results, on a keyboard. (You'd just have to learn the modes!)

replies.title: Re: Gregorian Chant Handbooks
replies.content:
For a keyboard, the modes are very easy. Play a C major scale: that's C Ionian. Now play the same pitches starting on and ending on D. That's D Dorian. Start and end on E and you have E Phrygian. Followed by F Lydian, G Mixolidian, A Aeolian, and B Locrian. You'll note that Aeolian has the same pitches as natural minor. The intervals between pitches make the mode, so if you want to play in C Mixolydian you'll have the same key signature as F Major, but your resolutions will be to C, not to F.

Clear as mud?

Of course, if you have sheet music, play the printed pitches, if you play by ear, just match what you hear, and you'll be playing in whatever mode your music is in.

BoysMom

P.S. 23 years since Freshman music theory, knowledge just a bit dusty!

replies.title: Re: Gregorian Chant Handbooks
replies.content: O Come Emmanuel is a song that comes to us from Gregorian chant.  It dates back to the 9th century.  It's also possible that O Come All Ye Faithful was a Gregorian chant, though it's more sketchy in origin.

comments.title: Reincarnation
comments.content:
Good evening!

I hope that you had a Thanksgiving full of delicious food, fellowship, and gratitude, and a Buy Nothing Day full of lazing about and empty of purchases. For myself, I baked a loaf of Universal Magical Bread to contribute to the family's feast and used Friday to finish the Republic.

1) I know ancestor veneration is not part of your practice, but I've been thinking more about what you shared about some traditions recommending commemoration of specific dead for ~30 years and then just "the ancestors" after that. Presumably, this is linked with the fact that at some point those ancestors get reincarnated and so are no longer around to pray to. This leads me to wonder, what kind of being/beings are ancestor-venerators praying to as "the ancestors"? Those ancestors who are between lives but have otherwise mostly shed their personalities? Some other kind of benevolent spirits that are not literally the souls of human beings whose genetic material you share but take kindly to that line?

2) Speaking of ancestors and reincarnation, do the same souls tend to reincarnate in the same family lines?

3) I guess reincarnation has been on my mind of late. You've mentioned before that consistent meditation often leads to memories of past lives. Is that something that you (or others) choose themes to try to bring up? Or is it more "when you're meditating on something relevant, the memories will pop up"?

4) I've been trying to learn more about different traditions of reincarnation. I knew about the Hindu/Buddhist tradition, and more recently I've been learning about Druidry's take on the subject. I was somewhat shocked to learn how little I had heard about the classical tradition of metempsychosis. Are there any other major traditions of reincarnation?

5) If I would like to explore reincarnation further, besides plenty of discursive meditation, any particular sources you recommend?

As always, thanks very much for your time and knowledge.

Best,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Reincarnation
replies.content:
1) "The Ancestors" appears to refer to a number of radically different beings. The creatures that showed up during an asatru rite were nothing like the ones I encountered during qingming. The answer to your question may very well be 'both'.

2) The only thing that I would add to JMG's comment is that there is some old Germanic lore about this happening. There are claims that the old Galdrkarls took steps to make sure they would keep incarnating among their descendants.

3) In Buddhism, trying to recover memories from a past life is generally regarded as a discount ticket on the crazy train. The general advice is to not be too attached to them. Fantasies usually fade with time while memories tend to become clearer and more focused, at least to some degree. It is always possible to obsess over a fantasy though, and convince yourself that it is a memory.

4) I personally would separate the Hindu and Buddhist traditions, and subdivide the latter. Hinduism has significantly more mythical material associated with it and a larger idea of how the great wheel fits in with the larger structure of the cosmos. Buddhism, by contrast, is fairly sedate about the whole thing but tends to have its commentary tightly coupled with deep insights into the structure of the self, mind, and memory. East Asia, independently of Buddhism, has its own traditions. Taoism and Chinese folk religion have some material dealing with how reincarnation fits together with divine rewards and punishments. More afield, I believe there are a number of middle-eastern gnostic groups who teach the same thing. The Bombay Zoroastrians claim that the original zoroastrians did believe in reincarnation and it is a negative impact of their Muslim neighbors that caused the Persian Zoroastrians to stop believing in it.

5) The Tibetan book of the dead, ideally with a hefty dose of modern commentary; deals extensively with this subject. Alexandra David-Neel's Magic and Mystery in Tibet features a number of stories dealing with it, usually with a side order of Tibet's idiosyncratic sense of humor.

I have no idea what you are doing, but good luck!

I’ve been slowly diving into the actual hindu side of things lately largely via the Mahabharata and plans to read the Ramayana. It’s beautiful work and feels authentic and wonderful.

Anyway there’s a bit of historical confabulation that happens regarding these issues because of England conquering India. Which is where a lot of those ideas entered the western mind rather than from some ancient source. The Theosophical society had a lot of ties to India and Hindu sources. So this idea that everything ties back to Hinduism is somewhat misguided in my opinion.

It’s also worth remembering that through out most of history and time the vast majority of cultures were polytheist. Hinduism just happens to be the source that most of this is filtered through and reintroduced to the west.

Just because the ancient Celts and Hindus both believed in reincarnation doesn’t mean they were worshipping Vishnu in stone henge.

Hermeticism posits that all religions are fallen off of the ancient/primitive/true religion.

So those two ideas taken together sort of feed this idea that you are having I would suggest.

None of this is to say that it’s impossible that the ancient Egyptians and Hindus were exchanging information or that it isn’t spiritually valid to “look for the source.”

It’s just that as we’ve hodgepodged together these systems of magic and polytheistic religions a lot of Hermeticism got used as all purpose spackle/plot filler.

So because of having to recreate and reverse engineer things we used the resources to hand. Which again is not to knock what the capable mages, occultists and polytheists have accomplished. But you need look no further than our hosts picture of William Walker Atkinson, “he of the countless pen names -- Yogi Ramacharaka, Theron Q. Dumont, all three of the Three Initiates who wrote The Kybalion, and the list goes on.” Let’s just say he didn’t pick Yogi just because.

replies.title: Re: Reincarnation
replies.content:
1) Some alternative possibilities:
a) The discarnate mindstreams experience time and space in a different, less linear way than we do, so from their point of view they may have many occasions to offer assistance to some of their descendants, while from our physical plane point of view, they will appear to have already incarnated.
b) The subtle bodies are composite in nature, and while one part moves on to another incarnation, another part remains to join the spiritual residue of the ancestors. Theories of this kind can be found in some traditions of Daoism and in Yoruba Ifa.
c) Similar 'partial reincarnation' explanations are found in African traditions to reconcile the continued existence of ancestors in the realm of the dead with the evident reincarnation of dead family members. Influences, features, or fragments of the consciousness of the ancestor are incarnated, not the entire soul.
d) I've read somewhere that among the Akan, joining the ancestors is the culmination of a series of human incarnations - those who fail to achieve their potential as human beings must go on to another incarnation, while those who achieve moral excellence join the ancestors.
This is similar to the non-orthodox Mormon theory of 'Multiple Mortal Probations' - multiple incarnations are necessary for human souls to progress to Exaltation and godhood, at which point it's no longer necessary to incarnate.
e) The 'ancestors' could include or incorporate non-human guardian spirits associated with families or lineages, or linked to particular places in the land where ancestral remains are located.
f) Certain magical funerary practices can allow a great deal of the personality of the ancestor to linger a longer time between incarnations and continue to strongly influence the living.

As with other esoteric matters, perhaps there isn't only One True Reincarnation (or form of ancestorhood), but multiple equally correct possibilities!

e) That seems very relevant for my own practices, as Germanic beliefs seem to have rather blurred the lines between ancestors and elves/dwarves/disir/norns/other spirits. Likely something that needs exploration and experimentation
f) I had maybe heard folks talk about some of these on here, but hadn't considered it. Thank you!

comments.title: Childhood mysticism, energy of gardens, the role of sacrifice, and reincarnation

Mr. Greer --

As you can see by the subject line, it has been a very busy week over here. Thanksgiving is one of the strongest magical times of the year for me, and so there are a lot of questions.

1) Reading Dion Fortune's work and her discussion of the difference between mystics and occultists, I found myself thinking of my earliest experiences with spiritual practice. My focus was on Buddhism, Taoism, and Hindu philosophy and meditation. Part of the intent was to calm myself enough to sleep (I have horrible insomnia, ever since I was a toddler), but also to try and understand the world in a larger sense. It didn't go great, but I kept trying at it consistently for multiple years.
It was only recently that I felt willing or able to try and work with the energies of the world, rather than simply observing them.
I'm wondering if this relates at all to the idea she put forward, of studying what you don't naturally work well with first, and then refocusing to what you work better with once you reach a solid background with the first.

2) Where I live, there have been two gardens established, ostensibly for the good of the community, where we can pitch in and participate.
Unfortunately, one garden was designed with no initial plan, and the other was set in an area with little consistent sunlight. The latter is a dead place, but the former is more peculiar. When I go into it, the energy of the garden feels chaotic, like there's a sort of sensation in my head like clanging cymbals.
My guess is that I'm feeling the sensations of a badly planned garden left to go wild and sorting itself out, but I would like your opinion.

3) I have had the fortune to be in many churches over the years, but no church has the intense energy of the semi-ruined St. Anthony's chapel. It is not only the oldest surviving church in town, but was also a Franciscan monastery for 109 years. The Franciscans dropped it when abuse cases surfaced back in 1989, and the property passed to the local financial crime family. I'm curious if the intense energy is more from the suffering that took place, or the spiritual power of the Franciscans.

4) I've noticed that many children go through a period of interest in Khemet during their early life. I'm wondering if this odd phase could relate to a memory of past lives, which decreases as time goes on. Given the extremely long span of Khemet's existence, there's a lot of souls that could remember where they were!

comments.content:
Just a quick follow-up to last week's 'Arberth'/'Narberth' question, looking at it from a straightforward toponomastic angle.

'Arberth' is the standard Welsh form of this place-name. It means something like '[place] adjacent to the thicket/copse/wood'. The prefix 'ar-' can also be seen in the place-name Arfon in north Wales, which is mentioned in the Fourth Branch. It means 'opposite/adjacent to Môn/Anglesey'.

'Narberth' is the form used in English these days. The Welsh 'yn Arberth' ('in Arberth') was interpreted as 'in Narberth'. This sort of misunderstanding could also happen during the copying of manuscripts.

Owain D.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Thanks for this -- yes, that would make a great deal of sense. Given that the texts of the Mabinogion are copies of copies of copies, that kind of scribal error isn't at all unlikely.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
And another quick note.

Should anyone be interested, the Dublin Institute for Advanced Studies has published English-language editions of most of the Middle Welsh texts that comprise the Mabinogion (only Manawydan fab Llyr, Culhwch and Olwen, The Dream of Rhonabwy and Peredur fab Efrawg appear to be missing as things stand).

They are amply supplied with notes about the various place-names and personal names that occur in the texts, if my memory serves me correctly.

Owain D.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
A few data points and a potential source:

1) Witch Hat Atelier is another Japanese work (a manga). It is aimed at pre-teen girls and is mostly positive and upbeat, but its central metaphor is that magic is art, and the growing conflict through the series is between witches that want to control everything very carefully, and those who seek the power of freedom. Possibly interesting since it uses "witches" as both the open and the repressed, though it doesn't much rely on the "witch archetype" that I can tell. (Also, the artwork is lovely).

2) Not that recent, but the movie "The Witch" (2015) goes very old school with baby-stealing, Satan-worshiping witches in the woods outside of an outcast Puritan family's homestead in early New England. It's very psychological, and while looking back on it, nothing jumps out as "whoah, that was super Jungian", the interplay between what Puritan society promises and what the witches do/take/offer has a strong shadow dynamic.

3) I haven't read it yet, but I've The Shadow and Evil in Fairy Tales sitting on my Kindle for a few years. It's by Jung's student Marie-Louise von Franz, and I suspect it might have some good material to work with. Also potentially relevant by her: The Interpretation of Fairy Tales and The Feminine in Fairy Tales.

replies.title: Jung, shadow, witch archetype

I recently read a diary entry by a woman patient of Jung's in which Jung conflated the witch with the Shadow archetype. The patient writes:

"During our conversation, Jung was saying that, "Mrs. X. may have a certain degree of pathology; she is a 'witch,' but that really doesn't have anything to do with you. It's your business if you fill her up with so much projection that she is damaging you!

You are each other's dark side, the shadow, a piece of unlived life.

She is scintillating, unreal, helplessly ungrounded. You represent for her a piece of young, real life, standing firmly on your ground, a happy wife and mother. This is why there is 'electricity' between the two of you.

But if you try to avoid her and run away, many other 'witches' will come your way. Fate doesn't let you get away without working on your negative mother complex." ~ Sabi Tauber: Encounters with C. G. Jung

Very likely this is not the only example, but it just happened to be fresh in my memory.

replies.title: (no subject)

One of the great challenges of visionary experience -- scrying and pathworking included -- is that by and large there's no way to check what you get, except in specific circumstances. if you're working with a particular symbol, and the imagery and ideas you get don't connect to that symbol no matter how thoroughly you meditate on it, it may have been astral static. If you get something that seems to relate to the material plane -- say, a prediction -- and it doesn't happen, then you know you got astral static. The thing to keep in mind is that you're in the early phases of a process of developing certain very unfamiliar senses and capacities. When it comes to the astral plane, you're in the same condition as the toddler for whom all four-legged animals are "goggie." replies.title: Re: Feet washing with cold water
replies.content:
Some further data points, as I've been experimenting with cold showers/baths longer than I've been learning magic:

I wasn't paying attention to the occult when I went through it, but Wim Hof offers a course where you start with short cold rinses at the end of the shower and work your way up to long cold showers and ice baths. The first time I took a 10 minute-long cold shower, I had a strong, giddy sense of euphoria for several minutes after, and felt energized and positive for the rest of the day, with some lingering effects for a few more days. His breathing exercises also seem to be very effective for energy and positive feelings, and they and the cold exposure enhance each other's effects.

Here's Wim's website: https://www.wimhofmethod.com/ He seems to be a delightfully enthusiastic and eccentric Dutch fellow, and you can piece together a lot of what his "Method" offers through the freely available articles and videos, but the video course was helpful for providing detail and consistency when I took it and has been updated since.

As for temperature, I live in Texas, so most of the year my coldest tap water is not all that cold. I definitely notice a bigger impact on how I feel (energy, mental clarity, positive emotional affect) from colder water. Once on a trip to Illinois in February, I followed my habit of turning the shower on to the coldest it would go, and let's just say that was an eye-opening experience!

These days I just start the shower cold and go as long as needed to get clean, but I try to make sure thoroughly rinse everything for at least several seconds in a row. Once a week, I take a bath as hot as I can stand for ~10-20 minutes and then a cold shower for a few minutes after.

I should likely point out that intentional exposure to temperature extremes can go badly if your body is not ready for it, and I avoid cold showers when I'm feeling ill and heat exposure when I'm dehydrated.

replies.title: Re: Feet washing with cold water
replies.content:
Try a full minute. When I started cold showers, I used a final cold rinse that I would count off to 60 seconds. I'd use it for all my actual rinsing; that is, I switched to cold as soon as I was done washing, and used it to rinse off all the soap and shampoo. I like to rinse thoroughly because my skin is a little sensitive to soap residues, so it easily takes the full minute and also guarantees the cold water gets all over.

Now (for going on two years) I just use all cold from the start. That has other benefits besides the etheric (e.g. no careful adjusting or accidental hot bursts; no condensation in the bathroom; less energy). You'll also likely not find it a difficult step from a full-minute cold rinse. Look for this benchmark: when you're standing in a full-cold shower and realize you've been thinking about something else.

Last summer during a visit to relatives in the south, I definitely felt like the cold tap water wasn't cold enough. It was like swimming pool water. I don't think a longer time compensates for that. Since there's usually no choice, I wonder if there might be other ways to enhance the effects when the water is in the seventy degrees F to body temperature range.

replies.title: Re: Queries for our generous host

comments.title: A strange data point

A question and (to my mind) a perplexing data point:

In last Magic Monday, JMG, you answered a post asking about possible adverse spiritual consequences of the “vaccine” thusly: “The thing that I've noticed is a weird lowering of the threshold of consciousness -- not just brain fog, but a mechanical state of consciousness in which words lose their meaning and anything outside a very narrow range of familiar utterances gets a blank stare and a mumble. In another post the same day you said something that stuck with me: “It's when you think that everything you're doing is utterly logical and reasonable that you know you've probably slipped off the deep end.” And: “The more convinced a society is that it's got sole possession of the absolute truth and anyone who disagrees with it is crazy or evil, the more certain you can be that the society in question is for all practical purposes stark staring nuts.”

My weird data point is this: I have two very dear and close friends who live together. One has been vaccinated, one has not. I am seeing and feeling that mechanical state of consciousness you described in spades…from the unvaxxed friend. (??)

I get a little of it from the vaxxed one, but if that dial is on 1, the unvaxxed person’s dial is on 11. In particular, any discussion about vaccines or masks that involves facts which might call the current main stream narrative into doubt has resulted in the unvaxxed jumping down my throat. I hear in the unvaxxed’s words that they feel they are coming from a place of logic and reason, but any attempt to point out that their logic has easily identifiable holes elicits very condescending behavior if not anger. replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Not JMG. Also more of a dabbler in dream things than an expert, so take the below as things I've stumbled across that seem worth further investigation:

If you are concerned this could be due to fear, the timeworn solution around here seems to be journaling to get to the root of the emotion.

I notice a lot of advice regarding dream work involves repeating something in the waking world often enough that it filters through to your dreaming mind. With this in mind, you might try coming up with an affirmation relevant to your problem (see https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/64539.html) and repeating it 100 times before bed, which sounds like a lot but with a short, focused, affirmation only takes a few minutes.

I have read in some lucid dreaming materials that staring at your hands can assist in stabilizing a dream. If you're still having trouble after giving the above a fair shake, it may be helpful to keep a dream journal focusing on what strange dream things you see right before becoming aware you're dreaming. Several times a day, call one of the more common things to mind picturing it as intensely as you can, and look at your hands with the firm intent to remain dreaming. You might take this further by putting a drawing of a common dream thing (or just a representative weird dream thing) somewhere and doing the above exercise whenever you see the drawing so you get the trigger -> response effect.

I've used the "see a thing in real life/do what you want to do in the dream" technique before to modest effect and in my experience it can take time to work, so give it a month or so of daily practice if you do try it.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Stephen LaBerge received his Ph.D. in Psychophysiology from Stanford University (1980). As a Research Associate in the Stanford University Departments of Psychiatry and Psychology he was engaged in laboratory research for 25 years, pioneering the field of lucid dreaming. He is the author of Lucid Dreaming and Exploring the World of Lucid Dreaming, and a co-founder of the International Association for the Study of Dreams (IASD).

comments.title: Magical alphabet

replies.title: Re: Magical alphabet
replies.content: All three of those -- the Malachim alphabet, the Celestial alphabet, and the Passing the River alphabet -- were created in late Renaissance Europe as variants of the Hebrew alphabet for use in talismanic magic. They work quite well -- but then so does just about any other unusual alphabet you care to use. If you want to work with Cornelius Agrippa's magical system, or Renaissance magic generally, they might well come in handy.

replies.title: Re: Magical alphabet
replies.content: Why did they decide to change how Hebrew letters looked?

replies.title: Re: Magical alphabet
replies.content: One of the odd things about magic is that you get better effects with something your mind can't interpret quickly. Incantations in a language you don't know work better than incantations in a familiar language. Sigils you can't read at a glance work better than sigils whose meaning is obvious. These alphabets get their value from the same thing -- if you've got a fluent reading knowledge of Hebrew, putting words of power in an unfamiliar script gets better results.

replies.title: Re: Magical alphabet
replies.content: Could you meditate on these alphabets?

replies.title: Re: Magical alphabet
replies.content: Sure, if that appeals to you.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: There's a claim in some of the old occult philosophical texts that in the third cycle of human civilization -- the one that's associated with the drowned continent (actually subcontinent) of Lemuria -- human beings deliberately made contact with the demonic plane and, deliberately or accidentally, allowed its inhabitants to get access to human beings in certain ways. The claim was that this is the reality behind legends like the Fall of Adam and Eve in the Book of Genesis, and all the other tales of the same kind -- and that this was also the point at which most human beings lost the ability to sense directly their connection with the higher planes of being. Dion Fortune, who discusses it in her book Applied Magic, refers to this as "the Lemurian sin;" I prefer to use a slightly fancier label and call it the Lemurian deviation. Whether it happened exactly as the occult texts claim or not, it provides a useful way of understanding certain of the problematic aspects of human consciousness.

The other day, my friend showed me a podcast in which the host fielded questions to the AI software GPT-3, a system that is apparently causing big waves in those concerned with AI safety. comments.title: Ritual work
comments.content:
I did some work with the druidry handbook and druid magic handbook a couple years ago; I resonated with and enjoyed that work, but fell off as I have young children and went through a couple moves and a job changes.

I have recently started up work with the heathen rituals you've posted (performing them daily for a couple weeks,) and I have questions...
There are quite a few; if this is too much for one magic Monday, I'll gladly ask again wherever you'd like to leave off.

1 - How does the LBRP differ from the SOP in intent and environmental? Both are clearing and protective as I understand them, but they certainly feel different; and they seem to affect my space differently.

2 - I've read the FAQ. With the guidelines for magic around children: does working a ritual outside help to dissipate the energies, so that the ritual work could be undertaken in a backyard in the morning and younger children (over 5) could play there later in the day?

2.1 - I've noted your responses to others about ritual work and disturbing nature spirits. My most consistent spiritual practice has been honoring of, and leaving offerings to, house and nature spirits. Would the benefit to children of moving ritual work like the MP outside be offset by the disturbance of the nature spirits?

2.2 - Is there a way to banish with exceptions (leaving alone those spirits who live in the space,) or to announce the ritual and give nature spirits the time to take cover, or perhaps even to "tighten the circle" of the LBRP so that it only effects my immediate space?

3 - I also have an ancestor honoring practice, focused on connecting with and honoring my well-healed ancestors. I note a lack of discussion of ancestor work in what I've read of your writings. What are your understandings of the nature/importance/lack-of-importance of ancestor work?

4 - I appreciate your teachings (in the druidry books and the Monsters book) on the afterlife and our various bodies' processes in death. My operative beliefs combine those understandings with other learning from my ancestor work. I've also noted that you recommend a singular pantheon for ritual work (in order to have the full "alphabet" of deities.) Do you similarly recommend that one's view, or vocabulary, of the afterlife match one's pantheon? (My view of the afterlife differs from the Norse pantheon I call on otherwise.)

5 - (Not a question) I’ve been working with your Heathen rituals to significant effect. In particular, my divinations are clearer after the LRBP and Middle Pillar, and I often have an energy boost similar to a cup of coffee (without the jitters.) If you have not checked it out before, I recommend looking into the Yggdrasil Oracle Deck as you work on your Heathen system. I’ve used it for research, meditation, and divination, and the results have been illuminating and edifying.

6 - I find that parts of the visualization work are easier for me with my eyes closed, particularly the light moving over the body in the MP. I also notice that the LRBP feels different now that I can see it with my eyes open. In the lore on these rituals, is there a significance to open eyed vs. closed eyed visualization?

7 - Is there an occult teaching about the sensation of choice? I have periods of feeling like I’m “riding” life which alternate with periods of a strong sensation that my choices matter, even to the point of feeling that something small like what I chose to eat or drink spirals out and effects the emotional life of my family. I generally (even prior to religious education) had a feeling that all my actions and even thoughts were being observed and I should act as though everything would be outed to the public. I have that feeling less as an adult, but it seems like the occasional sensation that my choices *really* matter is possibly related.

Thank you for this space and for sharing your understandings and wisdom. I've learned quite a lot just from lurking, and I look forward to learning even more as I engage in ritual work and participate.

Wishing you and yours well.

replies.title: Re: Ritual work
replies.content:
1) The best way to understand the difference between the LBRP and the SoP is simply to reflect on your own experience of them. That's all I'd be doing if I tried to explain the difference to you.

2) No.

3) It's not part of my spiritual path. Your mileage may vary, of course.

4) That's an entirely personal choice.

6) Nope. Do whichever one works for you.

7) Your perceptions are accurate. Sometimes your choices matter, sometimes they don't make a lot of difference. Divination can help sort out which is which.

replies.title: Re: Ritual work
replies.content: Thank you.
